Good, good.

I'm in New Jersey.

I'm in Rhode Island.

I an interview here, don't have to take the whole 45 minutes, but to give you kind of the agenda, I can give you like my sense of how I'm thinking about the role, we'll talk about you, and then I'll try and leave time for, I will leave time for questions at the end, we shouldn't take 45 minutes, yeah, but have you talked to anybody else before me, aside from, did you talk to Tucker?
Yes, I talked to Tucker.
Okay, awesome.
So the account strategist role, we operate today in sort of an agency model built on a product.
So our platform that we're building today is internal to the team's developing content for our clients.
Eventually, will be external, hopefully sooner rather than later.
And we will then allow, like, be able to

Put our clients into tiers in terms of self-serve versus the higher-touch agency model, but I think an advantage that we've had so far is the ability to kind of deliver for clients as we're improving the content.
And so we operate in like a pretty traditional, it sounds like a pretty traditional pod structure.
So and the titles are changing a little bit, but I think like the overall what we're producing and how we're producing it is staying relatively the same.
But we have managing editors or content managers that are in the platform day to day generating content.

And then the account strategist is kind of like the project manager who is close to what's happening in terms of deliverables, like keeping track of quality feedback and directionally are we moving towards the right KPIs and goals with the client.
And then you would work under an example.
Account director, who will manage like three-ish pods of account strategists and managing editors, content managers, slash content managers.
That's one of the titles that's changing, so I changed this.
And you'll be managing like three to five accounts as a strategist.

So the account director will think about like high-level kind of long-term SEO strategy, making sure like we're in line with AI visibility, opting into like experiments, changing direction where needed.
And you'll be really thinking about like, are we actually like on the right track in within that roadmap that the account directors built?
And so, and then connecting the dots between kind of the managing editor piece and like the larger interaction with the client.
So any questions?
Pretty straightforward, I think.
Oh, yeah, it's pretty straightforward.
Okay, cool.
been in that pod before.
Yeah.
Awesome.
Well, yeah, we would love to get your elevator pitch and kind of learn about you and your experience.
So I have been in content and SEO for around about like seven or so years.
And I started in-house at a t-shirt company with a really small team that I managed like five websites, eBay store, Amazon store, and even their Walmart store at that time.
I don't even know if Walmart has a store now.
So essentially, I would, I restructured the way that they thought about Amazon and how they were thinking about keywords in Amazon and how they needed to structure everything.
So Amazon didn't, you know, put them on the blacklist of basically keyword stuffing and such.
And then I also rewrote all of their meta descriptions, metadata, etc.
So that's kind of how I started.
Then I moved to the agency, worked there, learned everything technical about SEO, and was very much on the back end, setting things up.
Google Search Console, their analytics, creating those goals within the analytics, making sure that their GTM was set up properly, and those conversions were being tracked properly.
Constantly having to check in on that, just, you know, because it always breaks.
And then, you know, like redirects in place, those kinds of things, constantly doing tech audits.
And then I moved to conductor, and I was a SEO success manager, then turned customer success manager.
And I was a customer success manager in professional services.
So, yeah, those roles changed and, you know, promotions happened.
And then now I am working for iCrossing, which is an agency, a Hearst company.
So it's like Delish Magazine, Good Housekeeping.
I think it's Allure, too.
So those kinds of magazines.
And then also, so my main accounts right now are American Express and Boeing.
So I have multiple business units within American Express where I'm constantly keeping up on those projects, recommendations, making sure that their roadmap is in place.
I also have the account management piece.
We do not have an account manager for some of my accounts.
So I'm keeping track of all the projects and making sure that they're on point with.
So
In our own little pod, which is an associate director, I am an SEO manager, and another SEO manager and analyst.
So that is where I'm at.
I've only been working at iCrossing for about six months or so.
So yeah, that's pretty much it.
Did we find you?
Yeah.
I just wanted to see what the opportunity was out there.
I saw that it was fully remote, which is really appealing to me.
And the fact that it's a startup, and I feel like I could add a lot of value with building things out.
I was a teacher at some point and did a complete career shift.
So I used to write curriculums and organization and, you know, making sure that people were being trained properly.
And the same thing at Conductor.
I wrote a lot of their onboarding because I had a really bad onboarding experience.
And I didn't think that it was like...
like...
I didn't
And they didn't have the knowledge of building out a curriculum that was training properly on the platform for their actual customer success managers.
So I went and I created like a very like to the point training and I cut their training in half by like two months.
Wow.
Is that where your like passion still secretly lies and like training and process?
I like teaching.
like training.
I just like adding value.
I like being valued and just, you know, just creating something for that's going to be useful that people can use and we can grow upon, you know?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Okay.
Good to know.
I mean, like, I think you could, you know, like, obviously resume and everything fits this role, but also like I'm thinking about hiring like an onboarding specialist or something.
And so I'm like, oh, if you.
If you like growthx and you want to explore other options, it's just good to keep in mind.
We are growing very fast.
So that's, you know, it's a good thing.
So, like, thinking about especially these larger enterprise clients that you're supporting today, can you talk to me about, like, what the project management looks like?
Can maybe an example of coordinating different teams to get a deliverable out?
A lot of the deliverables that are being put out right now are by me, essentially.
But I manage the other SEO manager and the associate director.
They're just, you know, too busy to be adding things.
have to add them.
Not only, I have three different laptops right now.
So because of security, know, Amex is very secure.
And so is Boeing.
So I have to add not only projects within...
Our internal, you know, scope and making sure that we're breaking those tasks down and allotting those hours that are actually realistic.
You never want to over-promise and under-deliver.
So making sure that you're within that scope.
How much is the average time that this would take to get done with ad hoc requests and such?
So that is like, definitely, there's no process right now for them.
Essentially, Amex just sends an email and we get it done.
So I'm the one that I'm sitting.
Yeah, we got a couple of those.
Yeah.
So it's more along the lines of making sure that is in our tracking space, making sure we're hitting those deadlines, making sure that it can be QA'd internally before it goes out to the customer.
So it's up to the standard and that way, when it comes to renewal, they're not like, you haven't been producing.
Or you have not been producing exactly what we want.
So also asking questions to the client.
Is this what you need?
What kind of deliverable do you need?
And kind of creating an outline and being like, this is what we were thinking.
Here are our thoughts.
Would you like to approve this quickly?
And then we can get started on this project.
So also creating those outlines for projects to make sure that we're hitting all the points that they need to present to their higher ups.
Yeah.
So that is one.
also, project manager and analyst, make sure that he is creating those metadata, meta descriptions, all of the deliverables, tech audits, and things that you need to do with SEO.

And then also the UX team, like trying to get them and dev team, trying to get them involved and making sure that they are pulling their weight as far as implementing all of those things on their end.
And this is actually...
actually...
actually...
Like, through actual Boeing and actual Amex, where I am technically, like, I have an Amex email.
So I am in that computer making sure that, I mean, those projects within Confluence, and then for Boeing, it's Jira.
So there's a lot of different places I have to keep track of everything.
So it is kind of layered.
Yeah.
Did you say Amex makes you have a different computer?
Yeah, I have to carry three computers into the city every week.
20 pounds, it's a lot.
Oh my gosh.
Oh, shoulders must be.

Those stairs, though.
Awesome.
Super helpful.
Okay, and then, like, managing two giant clients like that, how do you, like, organize that?
All right.
priorities between the two clients and like juggle, like how do you structure your work week so that you make sure that you're set up for success for both?
So we have to hit a certain amount of hours, right?
So I structure my work week with the hours that are allotted per client.
And when we're setting up that scope and they have a request, I actually ask them what the priority is.
And if I know that my bandwidth is low for that week, I ask if this could be pushed out one week because or we could move another project to the following week.
And usually they're happy with that.
This is the priority now.
sorry.
Like we need to really get this done by the end of the week.
And just having that conversation and listening and making sure that you're delivering on time is one of the most important things.
So I make sure that maybe even pieces of the projects.
are being delivered per week.

So they know what kind of progress we're making and emails, sending out those emails to update them on those processes and those projects about maybe like on Thursdays, just so they're aware of what we're going to be working on next week.
So what is coming up, what has happened, and how long each of these things are going to take us to do.
Yeah.
Awesome.
And then I only have one other question.
So can you talk to me about a time when a deliverable was going off track and you had to turn it around?
Yes.
Yes.
So when I first started at this company, I, you know, you, you have to really understand the customer and what they need and business units.
We're really, really siloed.
So when I work with one business unit, they want certain things in a certain format and others in another format.
And at the time, there was a lot of transitioning going on because people had found other jobs.
And the reason I got hired is because, you know, they needed a person to fill that space.
Um, so when you end up transitioning, um, the lead on accounts, people get building trust.
It takes a minute.
Um, and having deliverables that are consistent with what they're expecting is super important.
Yeah.
But because there was two new people on the account and there wasn't a lot of direction, one of the deliverables, it was a PowerPoint, uh, recommendation and all of the direct.

The tried to get involved in giving feedback on the deliverable and then also the head of Amex, our contact, also had feedback and it was just going into a million different directions and each one was very subjective on what the layout should be or even the content within it.
So getting that on track, having those conversations, making sure everybody's on the same page on what's feasible and what's not.
It was one of those ad hoc requests also that was like, we needed this yesterday.
So we really needed to collaborate quickly and I needed to put something out there as quickly as possible.

So I just had to have a conversation, even though I'm an SEO manager, letting them know that getting multiple feedback is probably not helpful for a lot of people involved unless it's layered and it's not all at the same time, right?
Just making sure that you're getting...
Um, one person's feedback, especially if they have been on the account for a long time, and they know the exact expectations of what that client needs.
That's the most important thing.
And I was able to get that out on time.
And, um, and they were really impressed by it.
And they ended up, you know, using that to present to their higher ups.
Awesome.
hmm.
Um, well, as I said, those were all my questions.
Um, and since you're evaluating us as much as we're evaluating you, uh, what can I answer?
So, um, as far as the, like, what does success look like in the first, like 90 days to you?
I know that, like, onboarding can be a lot, and you have to get to know a lot of clients.
At Conductor, I had, like, 25, and they slowly onboarded me, like, one by one.
So I just wanted to kind of get some sort of idea on what onboarding looks like, what you think success looks like within those 90 days.
Um, yeah, so, um, pure retention is, like, our North Star metric right now, you know, like, uh, we just did our first year in existence.
So it's, like, our clients renewing, um, I sent out the first CSAT survey last week, right?
So, like, I'm sure that that will evolve, like, probably in your 90 days, how we're measuring that.
Um, but, like, are we meeting the contractual deliverables?
Are clients, like, Yeah.
Saying that they're happy with that, And like, not that they want to churn.
And that's like, you know, a very basic, what does success look like?
Like keeping your clients, we're not thinking, like, I think we're also very much in, as I said, like, we're changing kind of the role definitions.
And so I'm excited about somebody that wants to, like, take a part in defining what, like, the kind of what the day-to-day process of that maintenance looks like.
Our pods are, you know, in the spring, we brought on a ton of people and threw them in with zero onboarding.
And so we have a couple of teams that, like, everybody's getting it done.
We're all getting it done kind of differently.
And so I think, like, I would love somebody that wants to think about, like,
How do we, like this week I'm doing, I'm starting to build more clear, like jobs to be done in swim lanes.
Like how can somebody help build that definition, like all the way through the life cycle of a client.
And so that's one way that I would think about success.
And then one of the things that we measure about clients is just like relationship building.
So like, I would say if we were going to gut check or pulse check a lot of like how it's going, you know, are we, how are the, we talk to clients weekly.
So like, how are those conversations?
Are they seeing us as experts?
Like that I think is really, there's delivering and then there's like having a strategic conversation.
And that ability to engage a client and get them to tell you what's happening next, rather than just like checking the box.
I think those are the people that are really excelling in this role or the ones that are able to like build that really.
relationship and get that trust a little bit more.
In terms of onboarding, we have more of an onboarding process than we did in the spring.
It's still relatively short.
So it's like one week where you just are like looking, learning about like growthx and kind of how, like, why we exist, how we do what we do.
And then, yeah, it's three, like I said, three to five clients.
So you start shadowing probably one to two.
In week two, to slowly take over with like, to own that client longer term.
We're trying to have not like, as we have new people come on, we're trying to minimize like, other, too much change for an individual client.
So you'll probably be paired with a director that like, knows the client well, and can help with that process.
And like, join a pod where you'll

It can be a pretty smooth handover, but we're onboarding one to two clients a week, and then client onboarding is they go through a strategy sprint, so it's eight weeks of us calibrating content, making sure that we have all the things set up, and that's a separate team.
So if you were to get new clients, all of that stuff has already happened by the time an account strategist that's managing clients long-term gets handed a client.

So you kind of know who are the stakeholders, what's important to them, what does good look like for them, what's their definition of done, and hopefully all of that has been defined so that when you start to manage a client, it can be more of a continuation.
And less like, they're brand new to everything, and now you have to do all of this extra work, so we've tried to split up like onboarding for clients as well a little.
Yeah, and then I think, like, something that we're still working on is, like, that reporting piece.
So we're, like, writing a JD right now for analysts that can help.
Like, we have, we leverage, like, GA4, GSC, SEMrush, like, all those things for performance.
But would love to, like, be able to go a little bit deeper for clients that want to.
So that's still in development in terms of, like, success is, like, what other KPIs do we want to look at for making sure that we're going in the right direction and meeting clients' goals?
Okay.
So I know that GrowthX is mainly content building.
So how are you collecting data?
I know you're using SEMrush, you said, GSC.
Is it all in one place?
Is it integrated with the tool or...
Are we separately going into their specific?
Yeah, so we build, today we're building dashboards in Looker.
And so each client has their own Looker dashboard.
But we have an active project with the engineering team to pull that into our own system.
So I would say by the end of the year, that will be like, all of that will be pulled into our platform.
Okay, and then when, so from sales to set up, then it goes to the account strategist.
And then there's a briefing, I'm assuming, with sales on what was offered.
And like the scope of the work, I did see a lot of miscommunications happen from the sales cycle to the account strategist giving, you know, that onboarding presentation.
Yeah.
So.
So has that been an issue, or are you pretty streamlined with the process and offering and not having any hiccups with over-offering?
Yeah, I think our salesperson is the most collaborative salesperson I've ever worked with in my career.
He is like, this is what we're talking about.
This is the language that's in the contract.
Do you have any concerns?
Partially because our pipeline is so strong, like, in terms of clients that want to work with us, like, we say no a lot.
We're like, you're not, like, you're not going to succeed.
This is going to suck for us.
Like, you know, we have fired clients before where it's like, this is just a mismatch.
And, like, we weren't talking to the right people in the sales process.
So I think we have a really good, strong, like, system of checks and balances there.
And we have, like, editorial is our bread and butter.
And so, like, it's pretty standard.
Like, the strategy sprint team excels at, like, we get you up to five articles a week, and we know how to do that, and we know how to set the strategy.

And, like, so I think things outside of that, usually we pull in, like, an engineer or our, the chief of staff who was, like, originally kind of, like, an account strategist role will, like, help scope and things like that.

So that, because the core team is, like, really good at our core transferable, and so we want to make sure that if we're going outside of that, we're, we have the support that we need to make that happen.
Okay.
And do you have any account strategists, like, going into the client's office and, and kind of touch base, like, in person at all?
Is travel involved?
Not required.
think, like, we have one of our directors is in New York, and she's, like, gone to the, like, gone to lunch a couple times with clients and stuff like that.
Like, in San Francisco, we have a lot of, like, happy hours and breakfasts and stuff.
And I think, like, if the opportunity was right or, like, the timing on the engagement, certainly, like, there's the chance to do that.
But, like, also, it's, there's not, like, a travel requirement here.
Okay.
Yeah.
Well, I'm close enough to New York, so.
Yeah.
So that would be something interesting or something that I would want to do just because it does lead to higher retention.
Yeah, totally.
What is my last question?
Yeah.
So it just, it just sounds like you're growing.
And.
Yes, you are growing, and you just need systems and processes in place, someone to organize everybody, make sure that they're getting their things done.
And as far as like QBRs, and like, weekly, day to day touching base with the five clients, what does that look like exactly?
Is that because I'm normally an SEO specialist, would I be touching, have touch points with the SEO team, the content team, I'm assuming, and then with the director who would be above, correct?
Yeah, so the director is doing a lot of like the SEO work today.
Like, I think it depends on the pod, like where that falls.
But I think the pods themselves that we don't, we, we just hired an SEO strategist to possibly start doing that work, like outside of the pod.
But today the pods are like running that kind of on a maybe like bi-monthly or quarterly basis, kind of taking a look at like what are the other opportunities in the space.
And then, yeah, so like from SEO strategy, would be like within the pod, the directors will be setting that.
But like, I think it's if you want to do like the more that you want to do, the better, probably.
Like if you like that hands-on portion, I think that there's like opportunity to do that.
But it kind of probably also depends on the client.
Yeah, the client and bandwidth.
Got it.
Yeah, I think that's all the questions I have for now.
Cool.
Well, it was really great talking to you.
And hopefully you're interested in continuing.
I'm going to move on.
So, you know, keep chatting if you want.
Awesome.

Awesome.
What would the next steps be then?
So you'd chat, I think, with Bridget, who's my manager.
She's the VP of Ops, like Revenue Ops, and then there's like a panel.
Okay, perfect.
Awesome.
Yeah.
All right, well, it was really nice to meet you.
I hope you have a great rest of your week.
Thanks, Carly.
You too.
Bye.
